retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival recession describes the process by which the junctional epithelium of the oral gingiva recedes, leading to the pathologic exposure of the root of the tooth. [14] It is a clinical condition frequently seen in the general dental practice amongst patients with gingival disease. The gingival recession causes modifiable and nonmodifiable risk factors, as discussed in the following section.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians often have to decide whether or not to treat this condition surgically. Surgical correction of a gingival recession merits consideration when a patient is concerned about esthetics or tooth hypersensitivity, there is active gingival recession, or when the patient will undergo orthodontic treatment on a tooth with predisposing factors (inadequate keratinized mucosa, thin tissue-type). The two types of surgical procedures include root coverage or keratinized tissue augmentation. However, the benefits of surgical treatment lack substantial support in current literature relative to more conservative approaches such as management of the underlying etiological cause. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Complications specific to periodontal surgery include the possibility that the surgery may not achieve the desired outcome and the unique risks associated with each type of periodontal surgery. Pocket depth reduction surgeries typically result in some degree of gingival recession, leading to a longer appearance of the teeth and exposed root surfaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival recession may also prevent the complete filling of interdental spaces, resulting in unattractive open spaces known as black triangles. In cases of free gingival grafting surgery, the grafted areas may have a different color, leading to poor gingival esthetics. After the gingival recession, exposed root surfaces are also more susceptible to root caries, especially without effective oral hygiene practices. Pocket reduction surgery, particularly osseous surgery involving removing supporting bone in teeth already affected by significant bone loss, may increase tooth mobility. However, tooth mobility often improves during the healing process, as the gingival attachment, which contributes significantly to tooth support in severe periodontitis, is reestablished during periodontal surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Personnel may opt for other incision designs, instruments, and suturing techniques based on treatment goals, personal preference, and local anatomy. Variants of the gingival flap procedure differ by the number of incisions, locations of the incisions, and extent of the reflection of the flap. A notable variant is the modified Widman flap, which uses sulcular, submarginal, and connecting incisions for minimal tissue reflection and removal, resulting in minimal gingival recession. The improvement in attachment and probing depth is most likely due to the formation of a junctional epithelium. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases of gummy smiles can significantly improve with the available treatment alternatives, but the amount of reduction of the gingival excess depend on its cause. Some cases can be easily resolved with less invasive procedures, such as botulin toxin or hyaluronic acid, but these results only last for some months. Others require surgery, achieving permanent results.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pockets with depths of 7 mm show the most significant pocket depth reduction with periodontal surgery but also greater gingival recession than scaling and root planing. [30] Pocket depth reduction is similar for gingival flap and osseous surgery for pockets greater than 7 mm, but osseous surgery produces more gingival recession. [88] Regenerative periodontal therapy for infrabony pockets generally results in attachment gain and radiographic bone fill, with deeper and more narrowed angled bone defects favoring regeneration. Regeneration is typically similar to any regenerative material. [89]

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: An excessive gingival display is an aesthetic concern that interferes with patients’ self-esteem and psychological status. [17] Gummy smiles can be a cause of embarrassment, leading to patients hiding or controlling their smiles. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications include patient factors that may compromise treatment outcomes or increase the likelihood of complications.  Tobacco smoking is linked to a less successful reduction of probing depths and attachment gain after periodontal surgery and usually results in more significant gingival recession. [20] Diabetes mellitus is associated with a higher risk of postoperative complications such as increased swelling, flap dehiscence, and delayed wound healing. [21] Immunosuppressive medications and conditions may increase the risk of postoperative infections. Medications that interfere with angiogenesis and bone resorption may pose a risk of jaw osteonecrosis, although the specific postoperative risk is unknown. The risk is likely small but not zero in patients taking oral bisphosphonates and more significant in patients who have received intravenous bisphosphonates for cancer treatment. However, patients with medical conditions including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, coagulopathies, and immunosuppression such as HIV can undergo periodontal surgery, provided that glucose levels, cardiovascular function, and relevant laboratory parameters are within safe limits.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The success of non-surgical periodontal therapy is evaluated 6 to 8 weeks after scaling and root planing. [5] This re-evaluation aims to identify any persistent signs of periodontitis, including ongoing gingival inflammation, deep probing depths, continued attachment loss, gingival recession, or worsening tooth mobility. Based on the concept of critical probing depth, probing depths of 5 mm or less at the re-evaluation stage should be treated nonoperatively. In contrast, sites with probing depths of 6 mm or more will likely benefit from periodontal surgery. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Free gingival and autogenous connective tissue grafting may be challenging if the palatal vault is shallow, contains a palatal torus, or features thin palatal tissue. Pedicle flaps are challenging to perform for broad gingival defects and are unlikely to succeed if adjacent gingival tissues are limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Connective Tissue Grafts and Acellular Dermal Matrix Grafts
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival recession defects Increase gingival or mucosal tissue thickness

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- History and Physical -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal abscesses are classified depending on the location, duration of the infection, and their number. According to the location, they are divided into gingival and periodontal ; gingival abscesses are restricted to the gingival margin and interdental papillae, caused mainly by an object impaction [1] . Periodontal abscesses affect deeper periodontal structures, such as deep pockets and bone defects, usually linked to periodontal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Gingivectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Residual pocketing with excessively thick, fibrous gingival tissue but no underlying bone defect

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Clinical Crown Lengthening
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical crown lengthening is most commonly used to save teeth with subgingival caries or coronal fractures that extend below the gingival margin in an otherwise periodontally healthy environment. [53] It can also increase the available tooth structure for endodontic treatment and ferrule preparation. The procedure aims to reestablish enough space for supracrestal tissue to re-attach to the root surface coronal to the underlying crestal bone. Violating the space for supracrestal attached bone may lead to chronic gingival inflammation and gingiva recession. [54] It is possible that several months after the clinical crown lengthening, the gingival margin creeps coronally, which may trigger inflammation if a restoration is placed too soon after surgery or if the margin is extended too close to the crestal bone. [55] (see Box 5)

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Lateral Sliding / Pedicle Flap
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of single localized gingival recession or mucogingival defect with a normal amount of keratinized gingiva adjacent to the defect

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with both الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor). In addition, REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, indicating that an unknown‑type cesarean section scar and a poor reproductive history are both risk factors for preterm labor, while the unknown‑type scar also predisposes to early placental separation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: إنهاء الحمل - اختياري, which denotes an elective termination of pregnancy, is clinically associated with تهديد بالإجهاض, a condition described as threatened abortion. This clinical association indicates that the two obstetric entities share overlapping concerns, and that the presence of a threatened abortion may influence considerations regarding elective pregnancy termination in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1845
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that patients who have undergone a cesarean delivery may present with a scar of unspecified type, a factor that clinicians should consider when evaluating postoperative healing, potential complications, and future obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: scarring into a unified classification scheme that supports recognition, documentation, and management of pregnancy‑related complications across multiple languages and clinical decision‑making pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is categorized as a specific type of maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it is considered a subcategory under the broader classification of other conditions or status of the mother complicating these reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is relevant in the epidemiological context of several disorders, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and تمزق الشبكية (retinal detachment). Recognizing that the allowed qualifier pertains to the epidemiology of these conditions highlights the importance of monitoring their occurrence, distribution, and risk factors within populations, thereby supporting public‑health planning, disease surveillance, and targeted preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: High risk is clinically associated with نزيف مهبلي (vaginal bleeding), indicating that individuals identified as high‑risk may present with vaginal bleeding as a significant clinical manifestation. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of vaginal bleeding in high‑risk patients can guide diagnostic evaluation, risk stratification, and timely management to address underlying causes and prevent further complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN and High risk are both clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that an unidentified type of cesarean scar and a high‑risk patient status are relevant factors in the consideration, planning, and postoperative management of cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN” is clinically associated with “ولادة قبل الأوان” (preterm birth). This relationship suggests that when a caesarean section scar cannot be classified, there may be an increased likelihood of delivering before term, highlighting the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management in patients with indeterminate scar types.
